prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 42-year-old man.
I read online that Bell's palsy almost always goes away completely on its own within a few weeks. My cousin had it and didn't take any medication and was fine.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
- History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden one-sided inability to close the right eye and mouth droop fits a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy), especially after a recent cold. That is different from a stroke because you have no arm/leg weakness, speech or understanding problems, or severe headache. Bell’s palsy often improves on its own, but starting oral steroids within about 72 hours improves the chance of full recovery. Protect the eye (artificial tears by day, lubricating ointment and eye patch at night) to prevent corneal injury. A doctor should confirm the diagnosis, consider steroid treatment, and rule out other causes. Go to the ER immediately only if you develop limb weakness, trouble speaking/swallowing, severe headache, or breathing problems.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 90%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This sounds like a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy): sudden one-sided facial weakness that includes inability to close the eye and mouth droop, with no arm/leg weakness or speech problems. A recent cold fits the common post-viral trigger. Many people do improve without treatment, but short-course oral steroids started early (ideally within about 72 hours of onset) increase the chance of complete recovery. Protect your eye from drying or injury (lubricating drops, eye patch or taping closed at night). Watch for new or worsening symptoms (limb weakness, trouble speaking, severe headache, or confusion) which would be more concerning for stroke or other problems.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided facial weakness with inability to fully close the eye and drooping of the mouth, plus a recent cold and no arm/leg weakness or speech problems, most likely represents a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy) rather than a stroke. Many people do improve on their own, but early treatment (usually a short course of steroids) increases the chance of a faster and more complete recovery. Protecting the eye from drying or injury is important (lubricating drops and an eye patch at night). If you develop limb weakness, trouble speaking or understanding, severe headache, or worsening symptoms, seek emergency care.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden one-sided inability to close the right eye and mouth droop, with no arm/leg weakness or speech problems and a recent cold, most consistent with a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy). Bell’s palsy often follows a viral illness and commonly causes forehead and eyelid weakness (you note you can’t close the eye), unlike many strokes which usually spare the forehead and often cause other deficits. Many people recover, but early treatment (usually steroids) can improve outcomes and you need eye protection (lubricating drops/ointment and taping at night) to prevent corneal injury. Seek prompt medical assessment to confirm the diagnosis, start treatment if appropriate, and rule out other causes.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 90%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided facial weakness that includes inability to fully close the eye and a drooping mouth, after a recent mild cold, most likely represents a peripheral facial nerve problem (Bell’s palsy). Bell’s palsy commonly follows viral illnesses and affects the whole side of the face (including the forehead), whereas a stroke usually also causes arm/leg weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking/understanding. It’s important to protect the eye (lubricating drops/patching) and to see a doctor soon because oral steroids started within about 72 hours improve the chance of full recovery. Go to the emergency room right away if you develop limb weakness, slurred speech, severe headache, difficulty breathing/swallowing, or rapidly worsening symptoms.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided inability to close the eye and drooping of the mouth, with no arm/leg weakness or trouble speaking, fits an isolated facial nerve (peripheral) palsy—commonly called Bell’s palsy—and often follows a recent cold. This is different from most strokes, which usually cause arm/leg weakness or trouble with speech. Because the eye cannot close fully, it should be protected from drying or injury. You should have a doctor examine you soon to confirm the diagnosis, discuss treatment options that work best when started early, and get advice to protect the eye. If you develop weakness elsewhere, slurred speech, severe headache, or worsening symptoms, seek emergency care immediately.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden one-sided inability to close the right eye and mouth droop fits a peripheral facial nerve problem (Bell’s palsy) more than a stroke, especially with no arm/leg weakness or speech trouble and a recent cold (viral triggers are common). Many people improve on their own, but starting oral steroids within about 72 hours can improve recovery. Protect the eye (lubricating drops, tape eyelid closed at night) to prevent dryness and injury. Seek urgent care quickly so a clinician can confirm the diagnosis, consider steroids, and rule out other causes. Go to the ER immediately if you develop sudden limb weakness, trouble speaking/understanding, severe headache, breathing trouble, new vision loss, hearing loss, or a rash.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Likely a peripheral facial nerve problem (Bell’s palsy) because your entire right side (including inability to close the eye) is weak and you had a recent viral illness. Bell’s palsy often gets better on its own, but early treatment with corticosteroids (ideally within 72 hours) improves the chance of full recovery. Protect the eye with lubricant drops/ointment and tape or patch at night to prevent drying or injury. A stroke is less likely given no arm/leg weakness, no speech problems, and no severe headache, but it can sometimes present only with facial weakness, so evaluation is important.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden one‑sided face weakness with inability to fully close the right eye and mouth droop most likely represents a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy), which often follows a recent viral illness and commonly improves over weeks to months. Early treatment with oral corticosteroids (ideally started within about 72 hours of onset) can increase the chance of full recovery; sometimes antivirals are added. Protect the affected eye (regular lubricating drops by day, ointment and eye patch at night) to prevent corneal injury. Seek prompt care to confirm the diagnosis, start treatment if appropriate, and learn proper eye care. Go to the ER now only if you develop new arm/leg weakness, speech problems, severe headache, confusion, or worsening symptoms.

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): What you describe — sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to close the eye and mouth droop after a recent cold, without arm/leg weakness or speech problems — most commonly means a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy). It often follows viral illnesses and frequently improves, but early treatment with oral steroids (ideally within ~72 hours) improves the chance of full recovery. Protect the affected eye with lubricating drops, ointment, and taping at night to avoid corneal injury. A stroke is less likely because you have no limb weakness, numbness, or speech problems, but if you develop new weakness, numbness, trouble speaking, severe headache, vision loss, or balance problems, seek emergency care.
